name: apply
context: Execute a Terraform plan to create, update, or destroy real infrastructure resources
info: terraform apply executes the changes computed by a plan to bring real infrastructure into alignment with your configuration. It operates in two modes: automatic plan mode (generates and applies a plan in one step with confirmation prompt) and saved plan mode (applies a previously saved plan file without prompting). For production use, always save a plan with terraform plan -out=tfplan and apply it with terraform apply tfplan. The -auto-approve flag skips confirmation for CI/CD pipelines. Apply supports all planning options (-var, -var-file, -target, -replace, -destroy, -refresh-only) when run without a saved plan. After apply, Terraform updates the state file and prints output values. See [[terraform/plan]] for generating plans and [[terraform/state]] for understanding what apply writes.
body:
# Terraform Apply

`terraform apply` executes the actions proposed in a Terraform plan. It creates, updates, and destroys real infrastructure to match your configuration. This is the command that actually changes things.

## Basic Usage

```bash
# Apply with interactive confirmation prompt
terraform apply

# Apply a saved plan (no confirmation needed)
terraform apply tfplan

# Apply without confirmation (CI/CD)
terraform apply -auto-approve

# Apply with variables
terraform apply -var='environment=prod' -var-file=prod.tfvars

# Apply targeting a specific resource
terraform apply -target=aws_instance.web

# Destroy all resources
terraform apply -destroy
```

## Two Application Modes

### Saved Plan Mode (recommended for production)

Apply a previously computed and reviewed plan:

```bash
# Step 1: Plan and save
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Step 2: Review the plan output

# Step 3: Apply the saved plan (skips confirmation)
terraform apply tfplan

# Step 4: Clean up the plan file
rm tfplan
```

Saved plans are the safest workflow because you apply exactly what you reviewed. The plan file is a binary snapshot — Terraform will refuse to apply it if the configuration or state has changed since the plan was created.

### Automatic Plan Mode

When run without a plan file, apply generates a new plan and prompts for confirmation:

```bash
terraform apply

# Terraform shows the plan, then asks:
# Do you want to perform these actions?
#   Terraform will perform the actions described above.
#   Only 'yes' will be accepted to approve.
# Enter a value: yes
```

This is convenient for development but not recommended for production.

## Auto-Approve for CI/CD

```bash
# Skip the confirmation prompt
terraform apply -auto-approve

# Better: save plan, then apply (more auditable)
terraform plan -out=tfplan -input=false
terraform apply -input=false tfplan
```

**Warning**: HashiCorp recommends that when using `-auto-approve`, ensure no one can change infrastructure outside your Terraform workflow to minimize drift.

## Targeted Apply

Apply changes to specific resources only:

```bash
# Apply one resource
terraform apply -target=aws_instance.web

# Apply a whole module
terraform apply -target=module.vpc

# Force replacement of a degraded resource
terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.web
```

Targeting is for recovery and debugging. Regular use leads to drift.

## All Flags

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|----------|
| `-auto-approve` | Skip interactive confirmation (ignored with saved plan) |
| `-var='KEY=VALUE'` | Set an input variable (repeatable) |
| `-var-file=FILE` | Load variables from `.tfvars` file (repeatable) |
| `-target=ADDRESS` | Apply only this resource and dependencies (repeatable) |
| `-replace=ADDRESS` | Force replacement of a resource (repeatable) |
| `-destroy` | Destroy all managed resources |
| `-refresh-only` | Update state without changing infrastructure |
| `-parallelism=N` | Max concurrent operations (default: 10) |
| `-input=false` | Disable interactive prompts |
| `-no-color` | Disable colored output |
| `-json` | Machine-readable JSON output (requires `-auto-approve` or saved plan) |
| `-compact-warnings` | Show warning summaries only |
| `-lock=false` | Disable state locking (dangerous) |
| `-lock-timeout=DURATION` | Wait for state lock (e.g., `30s`) |

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `TF_VAR_<name>` | Set input variable `<name>` |
| `TF_CLI_ARGS_apply` | Extra arguments for every `terraform apply` invocation |
| `TF_IN_AUTOMATION` | Adjust output for CI/CD |
| `TF_INPUT` | Set to `0` to disable prompts |
| `TF_LOG` | Debug logging: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR |
| `TF_LOG_PATH` | Write debug logs to a file |

## CI/CD Pipeline Pattern

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
export TF_INPUT=0

# Initialize
terraform init -input=false -lockfile=readonly

# Plan and save
terraform plan -out=tfplan -input=false

# Apply saved plan
terraform apply -input=false tfplan

# Show outputs
terraform output -json

rm -f tfplan
```

## Post-Apply

After a successful apply:

```bash
# View output values
terraform output
terraform output -json
terraform output instance_ip

# View current state
terraform state list
terraform state show aws_instance.web

# Show full state (careful with sensitive data)
terraform show
```

## Error Recovery

If apply fails midway:

- Terraform has already updated state for successfully applied resources
- Re-run `terraform plan` to see remaining changes
- Re-run `terraform apply` to continue — Terraform only applies remaining diffs
- Terraform is designed to be re-run safely (idempotent)
- If state is corrupted, use `terraform state` commands to repair (see [[terraform/state]])

sections.title: Saved plan vs automatic plan
sections.body: Saved plan mode (`terraform apply tfplan`) applies a previously reviewed plan without prompting. Automatic plan mode (`terraform apply`) generates a new plan and prompts for 'yes' confirmation. For production, always use saved plans: they guarantee you apply exactly what you reviewed. Saved plans fail if config or state changed since the plan was created.

sections.title: Auto-approve for CI/CD
sections.body: `-auto-approve` skips the confirmation prompt in automatic plan mode. Use this in CI/CD when applying without a saved plan. The safer CI/CD pattern is: save a plan with `terraform plan -out=tfplan`, then apply it with `terraform apply tfplan` (no approval needed for saved plans). Always pair with `-input=false` to prevent hanging on variable prompts.

sections.title: Error recovery
sections.body: If apply fails midway, Terraform has already committed partial state changes. Re-running `terraform apply` is safe — it computes a new diff and applies only remaining changes. Do not manually edit state files. If resources are stuck in a tainted or unknown state, use `terraform state rm` to remove them or `terraform import` to re-associate them. For deadlocked state locks, use `terraform force-unlock <LOCK_ID>`. See [[terraform/state]] for state repair.

sections.title: Post-apply inspection
sections.body: After apply, `terraform output` shows all declared outputs. `terraform output -json` returns machine-readable JSON. `terraform state list` shows all managed resources. `terraform state show <address>` displays full attributes for a resource. `terraform show` displays the entire state (warning: may contain sensitive values). Use outputs to pass values to other systems (scripts, CI, other Terraform roots).

scripts.name: Terraform Apply
scripts.description: Run terraform apply with saved plan or auto-approve, variable files, and targets

TF_PLAN_FILE.description: Path to saved plan file (if omitted, auto-approve is used)

TF_AUTO_APPROVE.description: Set to true to skip confirmation (used when no plan file)

Plan should be generated and reviewed before applying
